title: Full-cloth case binding
subtitle: A hardcover binding covered entirely in book cloth from spine to fore-edge
excerpt: A full-cloth case binding covers both boards and the spine in a single continuous piece of book cloth. It is more durable than a half-cloth or paper-covered binding, simpler to make than a leather binding, and produces the firm, neat hardcover used in commercial library editions.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on case binding and cloth covering. Internet Archive scan available. Specific construction measurements consistent with Cockerell's case-making instructions.

glossaryTerms.definition: The pre-made cover of a casebound book, consisting of two boards and a spine piece covered with cloth or paper, joined but not yet attached to the text block.
The shallow channel pressed into the cloth on either side of the spine during casing-in, creating the hinge joint that allows the cover to open cleanly.
The step of gluing the sewn text block into the pre-made case, completing the book.

content.text: A full-cloth case binding has four main stages: sewing the text block, making the
case
casing in
, and pressing. The case is made independently of the text block, which is one of the structural innovations of the modern bound book: the two halves are built separately, then joined.
Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones. Use a fresh blade for long straight cuts through board. Always cut against a metal ruler held firmly with the non-cutting hand well back from the blade path.
Acid-free PVA is safe for indoor use without special ventilation. Clean brushes immediately after use with warm water.
What you need
Text block: signatures sewn on tapes or cords, with mull applied to the spine and allowed to dry. The mull wings (25 mm each side) will attach the text block to the case boards.
Boards: two pieces of 2 mm bookbinding board, each cut to the height of the text block plus 3 mm (head and tail squares) and the width plus 3 mm (fore-edge square).
Book cloth: a single piece wide enough to span both boards and the spine, plus 15 mm turn-in on all four sides.
Endpapers: the first and last leaves of the text block, or separate sheets of endpaper stock folded to size.
Making the case
Cut a spine piece from thin card (0.5 mm) the height of the boards and the exact thickness of the sewn text block plus mull.
Lay the book cloth face-down on the bench. Mark the board positions: two boards separated by the spine-piece width plus 5 mm each side for the groove channel.

Apply PVA to the cloth in the board areas and to the spine-piece area. Set the boards and spine piece in position. Fold the turn-ins over, mitring the corners neatly with the bone folder and craft knife.
Press flat under a weight for 20 minutes.
Casing in
Apply PVA to the mull wings of the text block. Open the case flat. Place the text block centred on the back board, pressing the mull wing down firmly.
Close the back board. Apply PVA to the front mull wing and press the front board closed.
Check that the text block is centred in the case with equal squares at head, tail, and fore-edge. Adjust before the PVA sets.
Clamp in the book press and press a groove into each joint channel with a bone folder. The
groove
defines the hinge line. Press with the boards open at about 45 degrees.
Apply PVA to the endpaper paste-downs and paste them to the inside of each board. Replace in the book press and leave overnight.
Finishing

Trim the head and tail if the text block protrudes beyond the case squares. Add cotton tape headbands at head and tail for appearance and structure. Title the spine in pencil inside the turn-in before final paste-down, or apply a spine label.
Care
Store upright between books of similar height. A cloth binding wipes clean with a barely damp cloth; do not soak the cloth or the PVA will re-activate at the turn-ins.
